source_title: Antonio et al. — ISSN position stand: creatine safety and efficacy
notes: Comprehensive safety review; addresses hair loss, kidneys, hydration.
full_text: The International Society of Sports Nutrition position stand on creatine concludes that creatine monohydrate at 3-5 g/day is the most effective ergogenic supplement for high-intensity exercise. The position stand explicitly addresses hair loss claims and finds no documented evidence that creatine causes hair loss or androgenic alopecia in healthy adults. Renal function remains normal in healthy users at standard doses.

source_title: van der Merwe et al. — Creatine and DHT in rugby players
notes: Origin of the 'creatine causes hair loss' claim; did not measure hair.
full_text: This trial in 20 college rugby players found that creatine loading raised serum DHT relative to placebo. The study did not assess hair loss outcomes. Subsequent attempts to replicate the DHT finding have been inconsistent.

source_title: Kreider et al. — Safety and efficacy of creatine supplementation (review)
notes: Reviews 70+ years of research; no support for kidney/liver damage in healthy people.
full_text: This comprehensive review covers over 1,000 papers on creatine monohydrate. Long-term and short-term supplementation (up to 30 g/day for 5 years) does not cause kidney or liver damage in healthy individuals, dehydration, cramping, or compartment syndrome. Side effects most commonly reported are weight gain from increased intracellular water and, rarely, GI discomfort during loading doses above 10 g at once.

source_title: Morton et al. — Systematic review and meta-analysis of protein supplementation on strength and muscle mass
notes: Identified ~1.6 g/kg/day plateau for fat-free mass gains.
full_text: Meta-analysis of 49 RCTs (n=1,863) on protein supplementation during resistance training. Total daily protein intakes above approximately 1.6 g/kg/day (about 0.7 g/lb) produced no additional gains in fat-free mass. Effects of supplementation diminished with age and were minimal in untrained adults consuming adequate baseline protein. The popular '1 g per pound of bodyweight' (2.2 g/kg) recommendation is not supported by evidence for additional muscle growth beyond ~1.6 g/kg.

source_title: Jäger et al. — ISSN position stand: protein and exercise
notes: 1.4-2.0 g/kg/day is sufficient for most athletes.
full_text: The ISSN position stand on protein concludes that daily protein intakes of 1.4-2.0 g/kg/day are sufficient for most physically active individuals to maintain or build muscle. Distributing protein across 3-4 meals at 0.25-0.40 g/kg per meal optimizes muscle protein synthesis. The post-workout anabolic window is wider than once believed (likely several hours, not 30-60 minutes).

source_title: Schoenfeld et al. — Nutrient timing and the anabolic window: a narrow window of opportunity?
notes: Total daily intake matters more than timing.
full_text: Narrative review concludes that the rigid 30-60 minute post-workout 'anabolic window' is overstated. Total daily protein and caloric intake are the dominant variables for hypertrophy. Provided protein is consumed within a few hours surrounding training (pre or post), exact timing is unlikely to meaningfully alter long-term gains in trained individuals.

source_title: Schoenfeld et al. — Body composition changes with fasted vs fed aerobic exercise
notes: No meaningful body comp advantage to fasted cardio.
full_text: Randomized trial in 20 young women on a hypocaloric diet performing one hour of steady-state cardio fasted versus fed. After four weeks both groups lost similar amounts of fat and fat-free mass. Fasted cardio did not produce superior body composition changes when daily energy balance was matched. The popular claim that 'fasted cardio burns more fat' refers to acute fuel substrate use, not long-term fat loss.

source_title: Tinsley & Bounty — Effects of intermittent fasting on body composition (review)
notes: Effects on body comp mostly mediated by calorie deficit.
full_text: Review of intermittent fasting research concludes that fat loss from IF approaches is largely explained by reductions in total caloric intake rather than any unique metabolic mechanism. When calories and protein are matched, IF produces similar body composition outcomes to traditional continuous calorie restriction. IF may aid adherence for some individuals but is not metabolically superior.

source_title: Lowe et al. — TREAT randomized clinical trial: 16:8 time-restricted eating
notes: Modest weight loss but ~65% from lean mass.
full_text: 12-week RCT of 16:8 time-restricted eating versus three structured daily meals in 116 overweight adults. The TRE group lost only slightly more weight (about 1 kg) than control, and approximately 65% of that loss came from lean mass rather than fat. The trial challenged the popular framing of TRE as a uniquely effective fat-loss tool.

source_title: Bonnar et al. — Sleep interventions for athletes (systematic review)
notes: Sleep extension reliably improves performance markers.
full_text: Systematic review of sleep interventions in athletes finds that extending sleep duration (typically to 9-10 hours per night) improves reaction time, sprint performance, free-throw accuracy, and mood. Sleep restriction below 6 hours per night impairs glycogen storage, increases cortisol, reduces testosterone, and degrades next-day strength and endurance.

source_title: Cheung et al. — Delayed onset muscle soreness: treatment strategies (review)
notes: DOMS is not required for hypertrophy.
full_text: Comprehensive review of delayed onset muscle soreness. DOMS is caused primarily by mechanical micro-trauma from unaccustomed eccentric loading, not lactic acid accumulation. Lactic acid is cleared within an hour of exercise. Soreness is not a reliable indicator of training quality or hypertrophic stimulus; consistent progressive overload matters more than how sore you feel.

source_title: Cairns — Lactic acid and exercise performance: culprit or friend?
notes: Lactate is a fuel, not the cause of fatigue or next-day soreness.
full_text: Review reframes lactate as an intermediate fuel rather than a fatigue toxin. Lactate is rapidly cleared and used by oxidative tissues. It does not cause delayed onset muscle soreness, which appears 24-72 hours after lactate has long since been removed. The 'lactic acid burn' during effort is more closely associated with hydrogen ion accumulation.

source_title: Wewege et al. — HIIT vs MICT for body composition (meta-analysis)
notes: Small advantage to HIIT, mostly from time efficiency.
full_text: Meta-analysis of 13 RCTs comparing HIIT to moderate-intensity continuous training in overweight/obese adults. HIIT produced marginally greater whole-body fat loss but the difference was small and not statistically robust. The main practical advantage of HIIT was time efficiency: comparable adaptations in roughly 40% of the training time of MICT.

source_title: Schoenfeld et al. — Effects of resistance training frequency on hypertrophy (meta-analysis)
notes: Twice-per-week frequency outperforms once when volume is matched.
full_text: Meta-analysis on weekly resistance-training frequency. When weekly volume (sets x reps x load) is equated, training each muscle group twice per week produces greater hypertrophy than once per week. Three sessions per week may offer a small additional advantage but the dose-response curve flattens. Frequency itself matters less than achieving adequate weekly volume.

source_title: Schoenfeld et al. — Dose-response relationship between weekly resistance training volume and muscle hypertrophy (meta-analysis)
notes: 10+ hard sets/muscle/week for maximal hypertrophy.
full_text: Meta-analysis of 15 studies finds a dose-response relationship between weekly resistance-training volume and muscle hypertrophy. Performing 10 or more hard sets per muscle group per week elicited significantly greater gains than fewer than 5 sets. Diminishing returns appear at very high volumes (>20 sets/muscle/week) for most lifters.

source_title: Kostek et al. — Subcutaneous fat alterations with unilateral resistance training
notes: Trained arm did not lose more fat than untrained arm.
full_text: 12-week RCT of unilateral arm resistance training. DEXA imaging showed similar reductions in subcutaneous fat in trained and untrained arms. Spot reduction by targeted training is not supported. Fat loss patterns are determined primarily by overall energy deficit, hormones, and genetics.

source_title: Behm et al. — Acute effects of pre-exercise stretching on performance (review)
notes: Prolonged static stretching pre-workout slightly reduces strength.
full_text: Review concludes that prolonged static stretching (>60 seconds per muscle) immediately before maximal-effort strength or power activities causes small but consistent acute performance decrements. Brief stretching (<30 seconds) within a dynamic warm-up has negligible negative effect. Static stretching does not reliably reduce injury risk in most sports.

source_title: Wilson et al. — Concurrent training: meta-analysis of the interference effect
notes: Concurrent training only interferes at high frequency/long duration.
full_text: Meta-analysis finds that concurrent aerobic and resistance training does not generally impair strength or hypertrophy adaptations versus resistance training alone, provided endurance volume is moderate. Interference appears with long-duration (>20-30 min) running performed frequently (3+ days/week) and close to lifting sessions. Cycling and shorter cardio bouts produce minimal interference.

source_title: Bloomquist et al. — Effects of squat depth on muscle and tendon adaptations
notes: Deep squats produced greater quad and adductor growth.
full_text: 12-week RCT comparing deep (120° knee flexion) and shallow (60°) back squats with load matched relative to depth. Deep squats produced significantly greater hypertrophy of the quadriceps and hip adductors, and greater tendon stiffness adaptations. The popular claim that 'squats below parallel destroy your knees' is not supported in healthy trainees with normal mobility.

source_title: Lauersen et al. — Effectiveness of exercise interventions to prevent sports injuries (meta-analysis)
notes: Strength training reduces injuries; stretching does not.
full_text: Meta-analysis of 25 trials (n=26,610) on injury-prevention exercise. Strength training reduced sports injuries to less than 1/3 and overuse injuries by almost half. Stretching programs alone showed no significant injury-reduction effect. Proprioceptive/balance training had a moderate protective effect.

source_title: Lopresti et al. — Ashwagandha and hormonal balance in overweight men
notes: Modest T increase (~14%).
full_text: Eight weeks of ashwagandha extract (Shoden, 240 mg/day) increased serum testosterone approximately 14% versus placebo in mildly stressed, overweight men aged 40-70. Effect size is modest and the population is not healthy young athletes.

source_title: Wankhede et al. — Ashwagandha during resistance training
notes: T rise alongside training, not isolated effect.
full_text: Eight weeks of ashwagandha root extract (300 mg twice daily) combined with resistance training produced a ~15% greater testosterone increase than training plus placebo. Strength gains were also greater. The study cannot isolate ashwagandha's effect from training.

source_title: Wolfe — BCAAs do not stimulate MPS alone
notes: Mechanistic review.
full_text: Mechanistic review concludes that branched-chain amino acids alone cannot maximally stimulate muscle protein synthesis because all nine essential amino acids are required. BCAAs raise MPS signaling transiently but do not produce hypertrophy beyond what adequate whole protein achieves.

source_title: Trexler et al. — ISSN position stand on beta-alanine
notes: Effective for 1-4 min efforts.
full_text: Beta-alanine supplementation at 4-6 g/day for 2-4 weeks elevates muscle carnosine and improves exercise performance, with the largest effects in tasks lasting 60-240 seconds. Effects on maximal strength or short sprints are smaller.

source_title: Guest et al. — ISSN position stand: caffeine and exercise performance
notes: Strong evidence across modalities.
full_text: Caffeine ingestion of 3-6 mg/kg 30-60 minutes before exercise reliably improves muscular endurance, strength, sprinting, jumping, and aerobic performance. Genotype variation produces small individual differences but does not eliminate the ergogenic effect.

source_title: Tambi et al. — Tongkat ali in late-onset hypogonadism
notes: Restored T toward normal in low-T men.
full_text: Daily tongkat ali extract (200 mg) for one month restored serum testosterone to normal range in over 90% of men with late-onset hypogonadism. The trial does not establish effects in healthy young men with normal baseline testosterone.

source_title: Trexler et al. — Citrulline malate and resistance training performance
notes: No clear performance benefit at standard pre-workout doses.
full_text: Crossover RCT of citrulline malate (8 g) versus placebo in resistance-trained men. The supplement did not significantly improve total volume, peak velocity, or muscle endurance across squat and bench protocols. Some prior single-session studies found small benefits but findings are inconsistent.

source_title: Harty et al. — Multi-ingredient pre-workout supplements (review)
notes: Most benefit attributable to caffeine.
full_text: Review of multi-ingredient pre-workout supplements. Acute performance benefits are mostly attributable to caffeine; other 'proprietary blend' ingredients are typically under-dosed or lack independent evidence. Long-term safety data is limited and the supplements are not regulated as drugs.

source_title: Kouri et al. — Fat-free mass index in natural and steroid-using bodybuilders
notes: Original 25 FFMI 'natural ceiling' study — limited generalizability.
full_text: Cross-sectional comparison of fat-free mass index (FFMI) in self-reported natural and steroid-using bodybuilders. Natural athletes clustered near FFMI 25; steroid users averaged higher. The study is foundational to the 'FFMI 25 natural limit' claim but has small sample size, relies on self-report, and is not a hard biological cap.

source_title: Parr et al. — Alcohol ingestion impairs muscle protein synthesis after exercise
notes: Post-exercise alcohol blunts MPS even with protein.
full_text: Small crossover trial in trained men found that consuming 1.5 g/kg alcohol after a heavy training session reduced muscle protein synthesis by 24% versus protein alone, and by 37% versus protein + carbohydrate. Even co-ingestion with protein did not fully blunt alcohol's negative effect on post-exercise recovery.
